join_order.notes: is_big[] dense char array size 60000001 (~57MB); O(1) membership test per orders row; accessed sequentially during orders scan so prefetcher works well
Dense array 5.7MB fully L3-resident; only ~2520 lookups total — negligible cost

filters.predicate: SUM(l_quantity) GROUP BY l_orderkey HAVING SUM > 300
filters.notes: Applied after Pass 1 merge; ~2520 qualifying orderkeys from 15M distinct
Evaluated via is_big[o_orderkey] direct array lookup; ~2520 rows survive from 15M

data_structures.pass1_qty_accumulator: dense_array(int32_t, size=60000001, ~229MB each) — EXACTLY 8 thread-local arrays (hardcoded, NOT nproc); merge reads 7×229MB=1.6GB at 200GB/s ≈ 8ms; versus iter_0's 64 threads producing 63×229MB=14.4GB ≈ 72ms merge
data_structures.qualifying_filter: dense_array(char, size=60000001, ~57MB); derived from merged qty accumulator after 8-thread merge; built in same parallel pass as merge; 57MB < 44MB LLC after warmup

pass1_reuse_optimization.description: SUM(l_quantity) per l_orderkey computed in Pass 1 is identical to the outer query's SUM(l_quantity) per group (since GROUP BY key includes o_orderkey which 1:1 maps to l_orderkey in qualifying rows). qty_merged[okey] from Pass 1 gives the correct sum_qty directly — no second lineitem scan needed.

parallelism.pass1_lineitem_scan: FIXED 8 threads (not nproc) for Pass 1 scan and merge. Each of the 8 threads owns one calloc'd int32_t[60000001] partial array. 8 threads scan 60M lineitem rows in parallel (each handles 60M/8=7.5M rows). After scan: parallel 8-thread merge — each merge thread handles a key-space slice of 60M/8=7.5M keys, reads from 7 source arrays, accumulates into tl_qty[0], builds is_big[] in the same pass. Merge data volume: 7×229MB=1.6GB reads + 229MB writes ≈ 9.6ms at 200GB/s. This is 8× less merge traffic than iter_0's 64 threads (14.4GB).
parallelism.pass2_orders_scan: Full nproc threads for parallel morsel scan of all orders columns (o_orderkey, o_custkey, o_orderdate, o_totalprice); each thread collects local Q18Result vector for qualifying rows where is_big[okey]>0; merge vectors after scan. 15M×24bytes=360MB at 200GB/s ≈ 1.8ms.

indexes.notes: File: customer/customer_by_custkey.bin. Dense int32_t[1500001], array[c_custkey]=row_index. 5.7MB, L3-resident. Called ~2520 times — negligible.

fields.name: o_orderdate
fields.source: orders/o_orderdate.bin (days_since_epoch; decode to YYYY-MM-DD for output)

optimization_notes: Root cause identified as excessive merge traffic from iter_0's 64 thread-local int32_t[60M] arrays (Category D — parallelism tuning). The merge reads (nthreads-1)×229MB per-thread-array: with 64 threads that is 63×229MB=14.4GB≈72ms at 200GB/s, which accounts for essentially the entire 66ms hot time. The scan itself (300MB lineitem at 200GB/s) takes only ~1.5ms regardless of thread count since aggregate bandwidth is fixed. The fix is to hardcode Pass 1 thread count to 8 instead of nproc=64: merge traffic drops to 7×229MB=1.6GB≈8ms, a 9× reduction. Scan parallelism stays efficient with 8 threads (each handles 7.5M rows sequentially). Pass 2 remains a full parallel orders scan (360MB≈1.8ms) like iter_0 — no sorted column, no index lookup, avoiding the correctness pitfalls that caused iter_3 to regress. Sum_qty is reused from Pass 1 merge array (no second lineitem scan). Expected total hot time: ~12ms versus 66ms baseline.
